---
title: "Week 7 — Table 1: Descriptive Statistics and Baseline Characteristics"
---

## Learning Objectives

By the end of this session, you will be able to:

- Understand why Table 1 is the most important table in a clinical trial
- Compute and present descriptive statistics stratified by study groups
- Use the `tableone` package to generate publication-ready baseline characteristics tables
- Identify imbalances between groups and interpret their clinical significance
- Choose appropriate descriptive statistics based on data type and distribution
- Create a Table 1 from a real randomized controlled trial (RCT)

## Why Table 1 Matters

In medical journals, Table 1 is the gateway to trust. Before reading your results, reviewers ask: **Are the groups balanced?** If randomization worked, treatment groups should look identical at baseline.

Table 1 shows:

- **Population characteristics:** age, sex, comorbidities, disease severity
- **Evidence of randomization:** no significant differences between groups
- **Generalizability:** who is included, who is excluded
- **Missing data:** how much is missing and in which variables

A Table 1 reveals whether your RCT answer applies to your patient. If your population is 85% male but your hospital is 50% female, your results may not generalize.

## Core Concepts

### The Structure of Table 1

| Variable | Control (N=50) | Treatment (N=50) | P-value | Standardized Mean Difference |
|----------|---|---|---|---|
| Age, mean (SD) | 65 (12) | 64 (11) | 0.72 | 0.08 |
| Male, n (%) | 42 (84) | 39 (78) | 0.41 | 0.15 |
| BMI, median [IQR] | 28 [25, 31] | 27 [24, 30] | 0.18 | — |
| SOFA score, mean (SD) | 8 (3) | 8 (2) | 0.88 | 0.03 |

**Key elements:**

- **Column headers:** Group labels (treatment group, control group) with N
- **Row 1: Variable name** — Clinical context (e.g., "Age in years")
- **Row 2: Summary statistic** — See table below
- **Row 3: P-value** — From appropriate test (t-test, χ², Mann-Whitney, Fisher)
- **Optional: Standardized Mean Difference (SMD)** — Effect size for imbalance (>0.1 may be concerning)

### Choosing the Right Summary Statistic

| Data Type | Symmetric? | Summary Statistic | Example |
|-----------|---|---|---|
| **Continuous (normal)** | Yes | Mean (SD) | Age 65 (12) |
| **Continuous (skewed)** | No | Median [IQR] | ICU LOS 5 [2, 10] |
| **Ordinal** | — | Median [IQR] or n (%) | SOFA score 8 [6, 10] |
| **Categorical binary** | — | n (%) | Male 42 (84%) |
| **Categorical >2 levels** | — | n (%) for each | Race: White 30 (60%), Black 12 (24%), Other 8 (16%) |

**Rule:** For continuous variables, check the distribution first (histogram, qqplot). If skewed, use median [IQR]. If symmetric, use mean (SD). If uncertain, present both.

### The P-Value Trap in Table 1

Statistical tests in Table 1 answer: **Are these groups different by chance alone?**

But here is the catch: after randomization, you EXPECT no significant differences. A significant p-value (p < 0.05) does not mean randomization failed -- it may mean your sample size was large enough to detect tiny random imbalances.

**Better approach:** Report standardized mean difference (SMD).

- SMD < 0.1: balanced (negligible difference)
- SMD 0.1–0.2: small imbalance (usually not clinical concern)
- SMD > 0.2: moderate or large imbalance (may need adjustment)

**Clinical judgment rule:** If your groups differ in a clinically important way (e.g., mean age 55 vs. 72 in an elderly trial), report it. Whether the p-value is 0.01 or 0.50 is less important than whether it matters to your question.

### Missing Data in Table 1

Always report missing data transparently:

```
Age, mean (SD)           64 (12)          64 (11)     —
  Missing, n (%)         2 (4%)           1 (2%)      —
```

If a variable has >5% missing data, consider:
- Is it missing at random (MAR)?
- Do groups differ in missing rates? (sign of data quality issues)
- Should you exclude this variable from analysis?

## The `tableone` Package

The `tableone` R package automates descriptive statistics and handles data types intelligently.

### Installation and Setup

```r
install.packages("tableone")
library(tableone)
```

### Basic Workflow

```r
# Create a TableOne object
# vars: list of variable names to summarize
# strata: grouping variable (e.g., treatment group)
# data: your dataset
# factorVars: list of categorical variables
# smd: compute standardized mean difference
# test: whether to run statistical tests (TRUE/FALSE)

tab1 <- CreateTableOne(
  vars = c("age", "sex", "bmi", "sofa_score"),
  strata = "treatment_group",
  data = my_data,
  factorVars = c("sex"),
  smd = TRUE,
  test = TRUE
)

# Print the table
print(tab1, showAllLevels = TRUE)

# Or save to file
tab1_printed <- print(tab1, showAllLevels = TRUE, exact = "sex", nonnormal = "bmi")
capture.output(tab1_printed, file = "table1.txt")
```

### Key Arguments

| Argument | Purpose | Example |
|----------|---------|---------|
| `vars` | Numeric/categorical variables to summarize | `c("age", "bmi", "sex", "outcome")` |
| `strata` | Grouping variable (optional; if omitted, shows overall summary) | `"treatment_group"` |
| `data` | Data frame containing the variables | `my_data` |
| `factorVars` | List of variables to treat as categorical | `c("sex", "treatment")` |
| `smd` | Whether to compute SMD | `smd = TRUE` |
| `test` | Whether to run statistical tests | `test = TRUE` |

### Advanced: Specifying Tests

```r
# Customize which test is used for which variable
# nonnormal: variables that are not normally distributed (use Mann-Whitney, χ²)
# exactTest: variables to use exact test (e.g., Fisher's exact for small counts)

tab1 <- CreateTableOne(
  vars = c("age", "propofol_dose", "gender", "complications"),
  strata = "treatment_group",
  data = surgery_data,
  factorVars = c("gender", "complications"),
  nonnormal = c("propofol_dose"),  # Use Mann-Whitney test
  exactTest = "complications",     # Use Fisher's exact test
  smd = TRUE,
  test = TRUE
)

print(tab1, showAllLevels = TRUE, nonnormal = "propofol_dose", exact = "complications")
```

## Exercise

### Part 1: Load and Explore a Real RCT Dataset

Download a real anesthesia RCT published in the last 5 years. Good sources:
- **PubMed Central (PMC):** Free full-text access to published RCTs
- **Open Science Framework (OSF):** Many trials post supplementary data
- **ClinicalTrials.gov:** Some linked to published data repositories
- **GitHub:** Some research groups share raw data with papers

**Example:** Search PubMed for "anesthesia RCT 2023 data" or choose from your institution's library.

For this exercise, here is a **simulated anesthesia RCT dataset** based on typical intensive care sedation trials:

```r
library(dplyr)
library(tableone)

set.seed(42)

# Simulate a sedation RCT: propofol vs. dexmedetomidine
# N = 100 (50 per group)
n <- 100

sedation_trial <- data.frame(
  patient_id = 1:n,
  age_years = round(rnorm(n, mean = 68, sd = 12)),
  male = rbinom(n, 1, 0.65),
  bmi = round(rnorm(n, mean = 28, sd = 5), 1),
  asa_class = sample(c(2, 3, 4), n, prob = c(0.4, 0.45, 0.15), replace = TRUE),
  sofa_score = rpois(n, 5) + 1,  # SOFA score: typically 3-10
  icu_admit_type = sample(
    c("Medical", "Surgical", "Trauma"),
    n,
    prob = c(0.5, 0.35, 0.15),
    replace = TRUE
  ),
  reason_sedation = sample(
    c("Mechanical ventilation", "Agitation", "Other"),
    n,
    prob = c(0.7, 0.2, 0.1),
    replace = TRUE
  ),
  # Assign treatment (randomized)
  treatment_group = rep(c("Propofol", "Dexmedetomidine"), n / 2),
  # Outcomes (independent of baseline -- this is a properly randomized trial)
  delirium = rbinom(n, 1, 0.25),
  mortality_28d = rbinom(n, 1, 0.15),
  icu_days = rpois(n, 10) + 1
)

# Convert variables to appropriate types
sedation_trial <- sedation_trial %>%
  mutate(
    male = factor(male, levels = c(0, 1), labels = c("Female", "Male")),
    asa_class = factor(asa_class, levels = c(2, 3, 4), labels = c("2", "3", "4")),
    icu_admit_type = factor(icu_admit_type),
    reason_sedation = factor(reason_sedation),
    treatment_group = factor(treatment_group),
    delirium = factor(delirium, levels = c(0, 1), labels = c("No", "Yes")),
    mortality_28d = factor(mortality_28d, levels = c(0, 1), labels = c("No", "Yes"))
  )

head(sedation_trial)
```

### Part 2: Identify Variables and Data Types

Before creating Table 1, document your variables:

```r
# Print variable names and types
str(sedation_trial)

# Summary of each variable
summary(sedation_trial)

# Check for missing data
colSums(is.na(sedation_trial))
```

**Questions:**

- Which variables are continuous, ordinal, categorical?
- Which variables have missing data?
- Does age look normally distributed? (Hint: rnorm() was used, so yes)
- Does ICU length of stay look normally distributed? (Hint: rpois() was used, which is right-skewed)

### Part 3: Create a Basic Table 1 (No Stratification)

Generate an overall summary table:

```r
# Variables to include
vars_of_interest <- c(
  "age_years", "male", "bmi", "asa_class",
  "sofa_score", "icu_admit_type", "reason_sedation",
  "delirium", "mortality_28d", "icu_days"
)

# Create the table
tab1_overall <- CreateTableOne(
  vars = vars_of_interest,
  data = sedation_trial,
  factorVars = c("male", "asa_class", "icu_admit_type", "reason_sedation", "delirium", "mortality_28d"),
  smd = FALSE,
  test = FALSE
)

# Print
print(tab1_overall, showAllLevels = TRUE)
```

**Interpretation:**

- What is the mean age?
- What proportion are male?
- What is the median SOFA score?
- Is there much missing data?

### Part 4: Create Table 1 Stratified by Treatment Group

Now stratify by randomization group:

```r
# Create stratified table
tab1_stratified <- CreateTableOne(
  vars = vars_of_interest,
  strata = "treatment_group",
  data = sedation_trial,
  factorVars = c("male", "asa_class", "icu_admit_type", "reason_sedation", "delirium", "mortality_28d"),
  smd = TRUE,
  test = TRUE
)

# Print
print(tab1_stratified, showAllLevels = TRUE)
```

**Questions:**

- Are the groups balanced on age? (p-value, SMD)
- Are the groups balanced on SOFA score?
- Which variable, if any, shows a concerning imbalance (SMD > 0.2)?
- Why might randomization have produced these small differences?
- Is p-value or SMD more useful for interpreting balance?

### Part 5: Handle Non-Normal Variables

Specify that `icu_days` (Poisson-distributed, right-skewed) should use non-parametric tests:

```r
# Re-create with specifications for non-normal variables
tab1_nonnormal <- CreateTableOne(
  vars = vars_of_interest,
  strata = "treatment_group",
  data = sedation_trial,
  factorVars = c("male", "asa_class", "icu_admit_type", "reason_sedation", "delirium", "mortality_28d"),
  nonnormal = c("sofa_score", "icu_days"),  # Use Mann-Whitney test for these
  smd = TRUE,
  test = TRUE
)

# Print
print(tab1_nonnormal, showAllLevels = TRUE, nonnormal = c("sofa_score", "icu_days"))
```

**Interpretation:**

- Is SOFA score normally distributed? (Look at the data or make a histogram)
- Did specifying `nonnormal` change the p-values?
- Which test was used for continuous normal variables? (t-test)
- Which test was used for continuous non-normal variables? (Mann-Whitney)
- Which test was used for categorical variables? (χ² or Fisher's exact)

### Part 6: Format and Export Table 1

Create a clean, publication-ready Table 1:

```r
# Capture the printed output
tab1_output <- print(
  tab1_nonnormal,
  showAllLevels = TRUE,
  nonnormal = c("sofa_score", "icu_days"),
  exact = "male",  # Use Fisher's exact if any cell has n < 5
  printToggle = FALSE  # Return as data frame instead of printing
)

# View
tab1_output

# Or save to CSV for manual formatting in Excel/Word
write.csv(tab1_output, "table1_baseline_characteristics.csv")
```

**Optional:** Create a polished version using `gt` or `flextable` for journal submission:

```r
library(gt)

# Convert to a gt table for publication
tab1_gt <- as.data.frame(tab1_output) %>%
  gt() %>%
  tab_header(
    title = "Table 1: Baseline Characteristics",
    subtitle = "Propofol vs. Dexmedetomidine for Sedation in ICU Patients"
  ) %>%
  tab_spanner(
    label = "Propofol",
    columns = matches("Propofol")
  ) %>%
  tab_spanner(
    label = "Dexmedetomidine",
    columns = matches("Dexmedetomidine")
  ) %>%
  cols_align(
    align = "center",
    columns = starts_with("p")
  )

tab1_gt
```

### Part 7: Interpret Your Table 1 (Clinical Reasoning)

For each variable showing p < 0.05 or SMD > 0.1, ask:

**Is the difference clinically meaningful?**

Example questions:
- If treatment group A is mean age 65 vs. B is 68, does a 3-year difference matter? (Probably not)
- If treatment group A is 60% male vs. B is 55%, does a 5% difference matter? (Probably not)
- If treatment group A has mean SOFA 6 vs. B is 7, does 1 point matter? (Maybe -- depends on the severity cutoff)
- If treatment group A is 10% surgical vs. B is 20% surgical, might this confound the results? (Possibly)

**Document your assessment:**

> "Groups were well-balanced at baseline. Mean age was 68 (SD 12) in both groups (p = 0.86). The Propofol group had slightly higher median SOFA score (7 vs. 6, p = 0.12), but this small difference was unlikely to influence outcomes."

## Common Pitfalls

| Pitfall | Why It Happens | How to Prevent |
|---------|---|---|
| "All my p-values are > 0.05" | Randomization worked; this is expected | Stop reporting p-values in Table 1 |
| "I reported 10 variables, 2 are significant" | By chance alone, ~5% of tests will be significant (1 in 20) | Use SMD instead; interpret clinically |
| "My mean and SD look wrong" | Data type issue: factor treated as numeric | Check `str()` before creating table |
| "Table is missing a group" | Forgot `showAllLevels = TRUE` | Always use `showAllLevels = TRUE` for categorical |
| "I do not understand SMD" | SMD is unfamiliar to many clinicians | Explain: "SMD < 0.1 means the groups look nearly identical" |
| "My medians are shown as means" | Did not specify `nonnormal` argument | List skewed variables in `nonnormal = c()` |
| "p-values are missing" | Forgot `test = TRUE` | Add `test = TRUE` when you want tests |
| "N is different across rows" | Missing data in some variables | Use `CreateTableOne(..., addOverall = TRUE)` to see missing counts |

## Verification

Before moving to Week 8 (hypothesis testing), confirm you can:

- [ ] Load a dataset and check data types with `str()`
- [ ] Create a `CreateTableOne()` object with specified variables
- [ ] Choose appropriate summary statistics (mean/SD vs. median/IQR) based on distribution
- [ ] Identify variables as continuous, ordinal, or categorical
- [ ] Stratify by a treatment group or exposure variable
- [ ] Interpret SMD and p-values in Table 1 context
- [ ] Handle non-normal data with the `nonnormal` argument
- [ ] Export and format a Table 1 for publication
- [ ] Explain to a colleague why two groups are (or are not) balanced
- [ ] Push your Quarto document with Table 1 to GitHub

## Resources

- **tableone Package Documentation:** https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/tableone/vignettes/introduction.html -- Comprehensive guide with examples
- **SMD and Balance:** https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5914752/ -- "Covariate balance in randomized trials" (Austin & Stuart 2015)
- **Table 1 in Medical Literature:** https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24688605/ -- Recommendations for Table 1 reporting in RCTs
- **Anesthesia RCTs with Open Data:** https://osf.io/ -- Open Science Framework; search "anesthesia RCT"
- **ClinicalTrials.gov:** https://clinicaltrials.gov/ -- Find registered trials; some link to published data

---

[<- Back to Basic Tier](../index.qmd)
